straight_beat.name: Hal Cifuentes
straight_beat.column: The Angels won yesterday, which is good. They hit 10 runs, which is more than enough, and the Reds looked like they were trying to catch a bus to the nearest park. But here’s the box score talking: the bullpen coughed up two in the eighth, and the Angels dropped their fifth straight at home. Wait, no—that’s not right. The Angels won, so the fifth straight loss is at home? No, that’s the other team. Let me get this straight. The Angels are 7-7, 0.5 games behind in the AL West. They’ve got a .500 record, which is okay, but you can’t win a division with a fifth straight home loss. Wait, no—yesterday’s game was at home, and they won. So the fifth straight loss is at home? No, that’s not adding up. Maybe I’m mixing up the numbers. Let me check. The Angels are 7-7, so they’ve lost seven games. If they lost five straight at home, that would mean they’re 2-5 at home. But yesterday

optimist.name: Sunny Escalante
optimist.column: The game started like a rally call — loud, chaotic, and full of promise. You could feel it in the air, that electric buzz that comes when the Angels decide to play their version of baseball. Yesterday’s 10-2 romp over the Reds wasn’t just another win; it was a reminder that this team is still capable of turning the page, even when the standings feel stuck in neutral. Sure, the record says 7-7, but

pessimist.name: Murray Doblinski
pessimist.column:
The Angels won again. Ten runs. Nine runs. Two runs. It’s the same old script, the same old Halos. You can’t help but wonder if there’s a scriptwriter in the front office with a pen and a notebook, scribbling “win by 8” in bold, because that’s the only way this team seems to know how to win. The scoreboard says 10-2, but the game felt like a high school playoff game where the underdog just happened to have a few more bats than the other team.  

The offense, for once, showed up. Shohei Ohtani hit a home run.
